manipulated_prompt_obj.content:
Provide one single-word clue that relates to some of your team's words from the list below. You can choose to how many and to which words your clue relates to.
The goal is to have your partner guess all of your team's words based on the clues you give them, before the other team has uncovered all of theirs. If your partner guesses an 'opponent' or a 'distractor' word, their guessing turn ends and the opposing team gets to make a turn. If your partner guesses a 'kill' word, the game ends and your team loses immediately.
The more words your clue relates to, the quicker you can win the game, but the harder the associations may be to guess for your partner, so choose your clue word wisely. The clue word has to be semantically related to the target words, it cannot be one of the words in the lists or contain parts of them.

Always give your single-word clue and your comma-separated list of related target words in the following format and make your answers as short as possible, never include any other text than is required in this form:

CLUE: <WORD>
TARGETS: <TARGETS>

Your team words are: back, scooter, ankle, cottonmouth, head, bus, rat, rv, anaconda.
Your opponent's team words are: copper, fist, titanium, sheep, horse, grenade, steel, bronze.
Distractor words are: car, trailer, aluminum, oxygen, cave, dill, sage.
The kill words are: walnut.

message.content:
CLUE: animal
TARGETS: rat, anaconda, cottonmouth
message.reasoning:
We need to provide a single-word clue that relates to some of our team's words (the "target" words) from our team's list: back, scooter, ankle, cottonmouth, head, bus, rat, rv, anaconda.

We need to give one clue. The clue must be a single word, and we also need to specify which target words we think it's related to (i.e., the target list to be guessed). Our partner must guess all our team's words (the target words we assign) based on the clues we give them, before the other team uncovers all theirs. The clue can be related to any subset of our team's words, presumably including all of them, but must be semantically related to those words, not to the opponent or distractor words (not allowed). Also, it cannot be any of the words in any list (team, opponent, distractor, kill words). Must be single word.

We need to choose a clue that covers some of our team's words. Possibly we could think of a broad category, like "vehicle", "animal", "body part", "reptile", "small", "big", etc.

We need to ensure our clue is not in any list, not contain parts of them. So cannot be "back" or "ankle" etc. It can't be "scooter", can't be "bus", can't be "rat", can't be "rv", can't be "anaconda", "head", "cottonmouth". Can't be "copper", "fist", "titanium", "sheep", "horse", "grenade", "steel", "bronze". Can't be "car", "trailer", "aluminum", "oxygen", "cave", "dill", "sage". Can't be "walnut".

But could be "animal", "transport", "reptile", "snake", "vehicle", "wheel", "body", "metal"? But "metal" might be related to opponent words (copper, titanium, steel, bronze), but that would be undesirable because the clue could also be related to opponent words, which is allowed? The rule says "Your clue word has to be semantically related to the target words, it cannot be one of the words in the lists or contain parts of them." It does not say we cannot be related to opponent words. But ideally we want to avoid connecting to opponent words because partner may guess those? But if partner guesses an opponent word (i.e., they think it's an opponent's word), it's a wrong guess and ends turn and opponent gets a turn. So we must ensure it's unlikely partner will guess opponent words. But it's okay if clue is also related to opponent words as long as partner does not guess them.

We need to decide which target words to assign.

Let's think of categories:

- back, head, ankle: body parts.
- scooter, bus, rv: vehicles (transportation).
- rat, anaconda, cottonmouth (snake): animal (rat is mammal, anaconda is snake, cottonmouth is snake).
- cottonmouth: also known as water moccasin, a snake. So that could be "snake". Also "snake" could apply to "anaconda". Possibly "rat" could be "rodent". So maybe a category "animal"? But then "bus", "scooter", "rv" are not animals.

We could do separate clues for each group (like separate clues for "body" or "vehicle" or "animal"). But we only can give one clue per turn.

Goal: we need to guess all our team's words eventually. We could aim to get all words in a few turns. The best is to cover as many as possible per clue, but also be guessable.

We could aim to guess all words in 3-4 clues. If we choose clue that covers all nine, partner may struggle. Possibly we could cover e.g., "vehicle" covering scooter, bus, rv. That's three words. "Animal" covering rat, anaconda, cottonmouth (though cottonmouth is snake, still animal). That's three. "Body part" covering back, head, ankle (three). So perhaps we can do three clues: first clue "vehicle" for scooter, bus, rv. Then second clue "animal" for rat, anaconda, cottonmouth. Then third clue "body" or "body part" for back, head, ankle.

But we must not use a clue that appears in any list. "vehicle" is not in any list; "animal" not in any list; "body" not in any list. But "body" might be a part of "back" - contains "back"? The rule says "cannot contain parts of them" meaning cannot contain the words in lists as parts. Does "body" contain "back"? No. Does "body" contain "ankle"? No. Does "body" contain "head"? No. So "body" is fine. Could also be "bodypart" (one word?), but we need a single word clue. "Body" is fine.

We could do:

Clue: vehicle
Targets: scooter, bus, rv

Clue: animal
Targets: rat, anaconda, cottonmouth

Clue: body
Targets: back, head, ankle

We might give only one clue now; perhaps choose the biggest group. However we may want to aim to use "animal" for three words; that is a good group. "Animal" is broad. Also "animal" is not in the list. And "animal" is a single word.

Alternatively "snake" might only apply to anaconda and cottonmouth (two). "Rodent" for rat only one.

"Vehicle" applies to scooter, bus, rv (three). "Transport" maybe similar. But "vehicle" is fine.

"Body" could apply to back, head, ankle.

We could also incorporate "back" in "vehicle" maybe also "back" could be "rear"? Not sure.

Let's pick a clue that covers maybe three or four words.

Potential clue "motor" relates to scooter, bus, rv. But "motor" is maybe too generic? It could also relate to opponent words? The opponent's "grenade" is not. "copper" not. It does not conflict.

But "motor" is a word but not in list. However "motor" is part of "motor" itself. But not part of any list.

What about "transport"? Not in any list. Could be "transport" for scooter, bus, rv. However "transport" is longer and maybe less likely to be guessed.

Alternatively "drive" maybe not.

Maybe "animal" is best because it's simple. And we could guess "animal" with three words: rat, anaconda, cottonmouth. That's straightforward.

Thus we can give the clue "animal". Let's list target words: rat, anaconda, cottonmouth.

Our answer must be exactly:

CLUE: animal
TARGETS: rat, anaconda, cottonmouth

We need to ensure no trailing spaces or extra characters.

But the instruction says "Provide one single-word clue that relates to some of your team's words from the list below. You can choose to how many and to which words your clue relates to." Then they specify output format exactly: "CLUE: <WORD>\nTARGETS: <TARGETS>".

So answer must be exactly that, with nothing else.

Thus final answer:

CLUE: animal
TARGETS: rat, anaconda, cottonmouth

But note that "cottonmouth" is a snake; but "animal" is a generic category, so it's okay.

Thus answer is ready.

We should consider whether "animal" could be ambiguous. Could partner also guess "horse"? That's opponent. That's possible if they guess "horse" they would be wrong and lose turn and opponent gets turn. However if we give "animal" for these three, partner could also think of other animals like "sheep", "horse", etc. But they'd have to guess exactly our target words. In gameplay, we would specify that we need them to guess exactly these three words. Usually they'd guess among all words they see. So they'd see "rat", "anaconda", "cottonmouth" on our team's list, they could guess "rat", "anaconda", "cottonmouth". They might also guess "horse", but they'd have to pick from the entire list (including opponent and distractor). The opponent words also contain animal categories: "sheep", "horse". So they could erroneously guess those. But it's okay.

We can also consider "body" for body parts, but maybe they'd guess "back", "head", "ankle". Could also guess other body parts like "fist" (opponent) but we hope not.

But we have chosen "animal" for three words, which is good.

Given we have only one turn, we want to provide a clue that covers as many as possible. 3 is decent. Could we cover 4? Could we find a clue that covers 4? Maybe "vehicle" also could be "bus", "scooter", "rv", "car"? Car is distractor. But we cannot incorporate "car" cause it's distractor. But we could think of "road"? Possibly "road" but doesn't fit.

We could use "metal" which is related to opponent words, but we might not want that. "metal" would be "copper", "titanium", "steel", "bronze" (opponent). So not good.

"snake" covers anaconda and cottonmouth only, leaving rat.

Maybe "rodent" covers rat.

But "animal" covers all three.

Thus answer ready.

Check format: "CLUE: animal" then newline "TARGETS: rat, anaconda, cottonmouth". Ensure commas separated with spaces after commas. Good.

Will there be any other requirement to include nothing else? The instruction: "make your answers as short as possible, never include any other text than is required in this form". So only those two lines.

Thus final answer.

manipulated_prompt_obj.content:
Provide a comma-separated list of up to 3 words from the following list that best relate or are most closely associated with the word 'animal'. Always start your list of guess(es) with 'GUESS: ' and do not include any other text in your answer.

dill, trailer, head, anaconda, steel, rat, aluminum, oxygen, sage, car, grenade, scooter, walnut, copper, bus, cottonmouth, bronze, back, titanium, rv, fist, cave, horse, ankle, sheep

message.reasoning:

But the prompt says "best relate or are most closely associated with the word 'animal'." Since "animal" is generic, we want the ones that are direct instances of animals (i.e., nouns that are animals). So we must choose three animal names from list.

Thus, choose "horse", "sheep", "rat". That yields three. Or maybe "anaconda" is also a specific animal. But we need three. Choose "horse", "sheep", "rat". Could also consider "cottonmouth", but that's less common. I think "horse, sheep, rat" are best.

We need to output "GUESS: horse, sheep, rat". Must be comma-separated list of up to 3 words. That fits.

Check if any other constraints: "Always start your list of guess(es) with 'GUESS: ' and do not include any other text in your answer." So we must output exactly: "GUESS: horse, sheep, rat". No punctuation after. No extra whitespace. That is all.

Thus answer.

Now double-check: The output includes "GUESS: " then the list with commas and spaces after each comma. That's okay. "horse, sheep, rat". Must ensure "rat" is spelled correct. Yes. So final answer: "GUESS: horse, sheep, rat".

The goal is to have your partner guess all of your team's words based on the clues you give them, before the other team has uncovered all of theirs. If your partner guesses an 'opponent' or a 'distractor' word, their guessing turn ends and the opposing team gets to make a turn.
